coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of BCNO quantum dots with applications for ion detection, chemosensor and fingerprint identification
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Xiaobo
Li, Lanlan
Yu, Jingjing
Gao, Xiujun
Yang, Xiaojing
Lu, Zunming
Zhang, Xinghua
Liu, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Boron carbon oxynitride quantum dots (BCNO QDs) with blue emission were prepared via the template of SBA–15 (a typical mesoporous silica). A modulated photoluminescence sensor was developed based on the different quenching effects of Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions on the luminescence intensity of BCNO QDs. The Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions have an interaction with BCNO QDs due to the electrons transfer between the BCNO and Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions, and the detection limit of Cu2+ or Hg2+ ion concentration can be as less as 10 nM. The BCNO–Cr6+ mixture can be served as a turn–on fluorescent sensor for detecting the ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect since overlapping of excitation and emission spectra between Cr6+ ions and BCNO QDs. Moreover, the BCNO QDs can also be applied to fingerprint identification and organic fluorescent films under ultraviolet excitation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
BCNO QDs
Ion detection
Chemical sensor
Fingerprint identification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and Composition of BCNO QDs
Absorption and Emission Spectra of BCNO QDs
Fingerprint Identification and Organic Luminescence Film Applications

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51771068 , 21603052 , 51671079 , 51771067 , 51571123 , 51671108 ), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province ( No. E2018202082 ), Financial Support for Scientific and Technological Activities of Returnees from Abroad (CL201606), Excellent Youth Foundation for Institution of Higher Learning of Hebei Province (No. YQ2014017), and Innovation Fund for Excellent Youth of Hebei University of Technology (No. 2015005). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM images of BCNO QDs, (b) particle size distribution of BCNO QDs, (c) AFM image of BCNO QDs, (d) typical height profile of the as–prepared BCNO QDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of SBA–15, SBA–15/BCNO composites and BCNO QDs after removing SBA–15. XPS spectra of BCNO QDs for (b) B 1s, (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s and (e) O 1s, respectively.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of BCNO QDs in water, and the inset shows the BCNO QDs in water under sunlight and 370 nm excitation. (b) Emission spectra of BCNO QDs solution under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Emission peak intensity of BCNO QDs solution under various pH values. (b) Fluorescence responses of BCNO QDs to 16 cations (100 μM). Emission spectra of BCNO QDs with different concentrations of (c) Cu2+ and (e) Hg2+ ions. Partial density of states for BCNO with (d) Cu2+ and (f) Hg2+ ion and inset shows the configuration of BCNO with Cu2+or Hg2+ ion, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of BCNO QDs with different concentrations of AA. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of K2Cr2O7 and excitation and emission spectra of BCNO QDs. (c) Emission spectra of the BCNO–Cr6+ mixture with different concentrations of AA. (d) The experimental data and fitting curve for emission peak intensity as a function of AA concentration.

Photographs of BCNO QDs–PVA solution on the banknote under (a) daylight and (b) UV lamp. (c) Amplifying photograph of (b) under UV lamp. Photographs of the dried BCNO QDs–PVA film under (d) daylight and (e) UV lamp.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of BCNO quantum dots with applications for ion detection, chemosensor and fingerprint identification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin Medical University, Tianjin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Boron carbon oxynitride quantum dots (BCNO QDs) with blue emission were prepared via the template of SBA–15 (a typical mesoporous silica). A modulated photoluminescence sensor was developed based on the different quenching effects of Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions on the luminescence intensity of BCNO QDs. The Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions have an interaction with BCNO QDs due to the electrons transfer between the BCNO and Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions, and the detection limit of Cu2+ or Hg2+ ion concentration can be as less as 10 nM. The BCNO–Cr6+ mixture can be served as a turn–on fluorescent sensor for detecting the ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect since overlapping of excitation and emission spectra between Cr6+ ions and BCNO QDs. Moreover, the BCNO QDs can also be applied to fingerprint identification and organic fluorescent films under ultraviolet excitation.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescence materials have been widely used in white light emitting diodes (LEDs), displays, fluorescence labeling, and biomedical areas [1–3]. Various luminescence materials have been developed such as rare earth doped phosphors [4–6], quantum dots [7–10], carbon based [11–13], and boron nitride based (BN, BCN, BCNO) luminescence materials [14–16]. Quantum dots have been recognized as excellent candidates for the next–generation lighting and display. Different series of QDs, including CdSe, CdS, CuInS, CsPbX3, carbon and graphene dots, have been explored for QD–based LEDs (QLEDs) [7–9,11,12]. In addition, QDs have great potential applications in ion detection, chemical sensors and biosensor. Water soluble QDs with their surfaces coated with ample carboxyl or amine groups are easier to conjugate biomolecules or special functional groups, which is in favor of application for QDs as fluorescent probes [17]. Water soluble CdS QDs capped by polyphosphate, L-cysteine or thioglycerol were applied to selective ion detection and the detection limit was about 0.1 μM for Cu2+ ion [18]. Wu and Yan [19] applied Ni2+–modulated homocysteine–capped CdTe QDs as a turn-on photoluminescence sensor for detecting histidine in biological fluids with the detection limit of 0.3 μM. D. Pan et al. [20] developed a novel eco-friendly approach to prepared grapheme quantum dots from coffee grounds with poly(ethylene imine) functionalization for Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions detection. N. Zarghami et al. [21] reported a simple strategy for sensitive detection of telomerase activity using liposome-encapsulated cadmium telluride QDs. O. Akinloye et al. [22] developed a novel peptide-based fluorescence-based biosensor for detection and measurement of negatively charged engineered nanoparticles (ZnO, Fe3O4, CeO2). In order to achieve ion detection in aqueous phase, the semiconductor QDs were usually needed to cap organic functional groups to realize aqueous dispersion. Carbon dots were also applied to ion detection [23] and fingerprint identification [24], which have good performance in chemical sensors. However, the ion detection mechanism especially the electron transition process between the quantum dots and ions are still needed to deeply investigate. As an important category of layer materials analogous to graphene, hexagonal boron nitride (BN) has attracted increasing attention due to its fascinating physical properties such as unique optical features, promising dielectric performance, high thermal conductivity and chemical stability [25–27]. Hexagonal BN QDs have been successfully prepared and show very good performance in cells imaging and biomedical areas [28–30]. Up to now, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report about BN QDs for further investigation besides cells imaging and biomedical applications. In addition, the applications of BN based QDs in ion detection, chemosensor, and organic luminescence films are imperative to develop, compared with other luminescence QDs [31–33].

BCNO phosphor is a typical non–rare earth doped BN based luminescence material, which was first prepared by T. Ogi with combustion method in 2008 [16]. BCNO phosphors have been attracted much attention due to its excellent characteristics: free rare–earth ions, no toxicity and pollution, wide range of excitation (ultraviolet to blue light) and emission spectra (blue to red light), and high quantum efficiency [16,34,35]. BCNO phosphors have great potential applications in white LEDs, display, phosphorus pigment, DNA labeling, and biological imaging [36]. At present, BCNO phosphors have been synthesized with various methods, such as solid state reaction method, microwave method, the molten salt growth method, electrospinning and liquid phase method [34,37–41]. Different sizes and shapes of BCNO phosphors have been obtained with above methods. Micron sized BCNO particles were synthesized by combustion, microwave or liquid phase method, and BCNO nanofibers with a diameter of 200 nm were obtained via eletrospinning [40]. BCN nanosheets and BCNO nanoparticles were prepared by Lei using salt melt method [35]. In addition, the emission spectra of BCNO phosphors can be modulated by carbon sources, polymer decomposition temperature and doping elements [42–44]. Up to now, BCNO QDs have remained much less explored and it still remains challenges for their facile preparations and further investigation. Very recently, C. Zhi et al. [45] prepared blue fluorescent monolayer BN and BCNO QDs for bio–imaging probes, which was significant for the development of BN based QDs. According to the reports, there are abundant chemical bonds in BCNO phosphors including BN, BO, CN, CC, CO, CH, NH, and OH bonds, which have important influences on absorption and emission properties [46–48]. In addition, there are more chemical bonds in surface states and large specific surface of nanoparticles for BCNO QDs which are more sensitive to some specific ions. Therefore, BCNO QDs may have excellent sensitivity and selectivity for chemical or biological sensors due to its special structure and surface chemical bonds.

In this paper, we have successfully synthesized blue emitting BCNO QDs with 4.5 ± 0.9 nm via the template of SBA–15. BCNO QDs have very good dispersion in water without further surface functionalization. In addition, we investigated the fluorescent properties between the BCNO QDs and metal ions via theoretical and experimental study, and found that Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions had the fluorescence quenching effects on BCNO QDs, which was induced by electrons transfer process between Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions with BCNO QDs. Moreover, we fabricated BCNO–Cr6+ based fluorescent chemosensor for the detection of ascorbic acid, and the photoluminescence of BCNO QDs can be turned–off by the Cr6+ and turned on by adding the ascorbic acid. Furthermore, the fingerprint identification and luminescence polymer films were also realized using BCNO QDs under ultraviolet light.

All the calculations were carried out using the spin–polarized DFT method implemented in the DMol3 code [49,50]. Full geometry optimizations without symmetry constraints were performed employing the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange–correlation functional [51] with Grimme's dispersion correction [52]. A double numerical plus polarization (DNP) basis set [49] with a global orbital cutoff of 4.6 Å was adopted. For the core treatment, we used the most accurate all electron relativistic method [53], which considered all core electrons explicitly and introduced relativistic effects into the core. The self–consistent field (SCF) procedure was used with a convergence threshold of 10−6 au on the energy and electron density. For geometry optimization, the convergence thresholds of the energy, the maximum force, and the maximum displacement were set to 10−6 Ha, 0.001 Ha/Å and 0.005 Å, respectively.

We used a finite BN nanoflake with the edges passivated by hydrogen atoms to represent a BN sheet. The PBE–D/DNP optimized BN bond length within central region of BN nanoflake region is 1.45 Å, in line with the literature value for the bulk hexagonal BN (1.45 Å) [54]. BCNO nanoflake was then created by the substitution of single N with C and O atom from BN nanoflake as shown in Fig. 5, and the geometries were subsequently optimized with all the relaxed atom coordinates. The interaction between BCNO central region and metal ions was investigated. The metal ions were initially located above C atom and the geometry was fully relaxed. The partial density of states (PDOS) and total density of states (TDOS) of BCNO with metal ions were obtained to investigate the electrons transfer process between BCNO and metal ions.

SBA–15 was used as template and reaction vessel with pore size of 7 nm, and the morphology of SBA–15 was shown in Fig. S1. Boric acid (H3BO3), urea (CH4NO2) and citric acid (C6H8O7) were used as boron, nitrogen and carbon sources, respectively. Initially, 0.01 mol boric acid, 0.05 mol urea and 0.002 mol citric acid were added into 20 mL ethyl alcohol, and it was heated to 60 °C to form a clear transparent solution. Then, 0.25 g SBA–15 was added into the above solution and stirred vigorously until the ethyl alcohol was evaporated to obtain precursor, and the precursor was sintered at 650 °C for 2 h under an air condition to obtain SBA–15/BCNO composites. The purity of all the raw materials is analytical pure.

Firstly, SBA–15/BCNO composites were washed twice with hot water to remove the redundant B2O3. The SBA–15 template was removed by 4% HF solution, and the residual HF was neutralized by CaCO3 and CaF2 precipitation was formed. The BCNO solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm (revolutions per minute) to remove the CaF2 precipitation and supernatant was lyophilized by a freeze dryer, and BCNO QDs were obtained.

Different concentrations of metal ion solution with same BCNO QDs contents (1.6 mg/mL) were prepared by controlling volume of CuCl2, FeCl3, AlCl3, MgCl2, Zn(CH3COO)2, CaCl2, KCl, CrCl3, NiCl2, NH4Cl, BaCl2, AgNO3, MnCl2, PbCl2, CoCl2 or HgCl2 solutions (1 mM). Simultaneously, different volumes of deionized water were added so that the mixed solution of BCNO QDs and metal ions kept the same volume. The metal ion concentration in the mixed solution was fixed at 100 μM (μmol/L). A series of CuCl2, HgCl2 and K2Cr2O7 solutions with different Cu2+ and Hg2+ ion concentrations (0, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 500 μM) and various Cr6+ concentrations (0–500 μM) with same BCNO QDs contents were also prepared.

0.01 g BCNO QDs were dispersed in 1 mL distilled water, and 0.1 g PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) was added into the solution and it was heated to 80 °C to dissolve the PVA. The above solution was smeared on the finger, and pressed on the banknote, and a flexible film was obtained after volatilizing the water, which was used for fingerprint identification under ultraviolet irradiation.

The microstructure of BCNO QDs was measured by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL 2100). The thickness and morphology of BCNO QDs on quartz substrate were measured by an atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker, Nanoscope V). X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI1600EXCA) was used to characterize the chemical states of BCNO QDs. The chemical binding status was measured via fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Bruker, WQF–410). The excitation spectra, emission spectra, quantum efficiency and decay curves of BCNO QDs were measured by a steady and transient state spectrophotometer (Horiba, FL–3–22). The quantum efficiency of BCNO QDs solid was measured by an absolute method with an integrating sphere accessory of FL–3–22. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were performed by a spectrophotometer (Hitachi, U–3900H).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and Composition of BCNO QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure and morphology of BCNO QDs were characterized by TEM and AFM images, as shown in Fig. 1 . It can be seen from the TEM images (Fig. 1(a) and Fig. S2) that BCNO QDs are uniform in size and their sizes are in the range of 3–7 nm, with an average diameter of 4.5 ± 0.9 nm (Fig. 1(b)). The lattice fringe spacing of BCNO QDs is about 0.216 nm, corresponding to the (100) planes of BN crystal [29,30]. AFM image (Fig. 1(c)) shows the thickness and morphology of BCNO QDs, of which the height is about 5.0 nm, and BCNO QDs have good dispersion on the quartz substrate.

Fig. 2(a) shows the FTIR spectra of the SBA–15, SBA–15/BCNO composites and BCNO QDs after removing SBA–15. The SBA–15 has two typical stretching vibration peaks centered at 500 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1 which are assigned to SiO bonds. The SBA–15/BCNO composites not only have the SiO bonds, but also have BNB (~800 cm−1), OBN (~1200 cm−1), BN (~1400 cm−1), and CN (~1650 cm−1) bonds. After removing the SBA–15 template, there are no obvious stretching vibration peaks of SiO bonds and it remains the typical stretching vibration peaks of BCNO QDs [39–41]. We also investigated the N2 absorption/desorption isotherms for SBA–15 and SBA–15/BCNO composites (Fig. S3). The results indicate that the SBA–15 has a large specific surface area (766.497 cm3·g−1), and the specific surface area of SBA–15/BCNO composites is decreased to 167.511 cm3·g−1 as the BCNO QDs are formed in the pore of SBA–15 template. Fig. 2(b)–(e) show the XPS spectra of BCNO QDs, which suggests that the prepared BCNO QDs are consisted of B, C, N, and O elements. The B 1s spectrum can be fitted by two Gaussian curves centered at 192.2 eV and 194.2 eV which is originated from the BO and BN bonds, respectively [40]. The C 1s spectrum is fitted well with three components centered at 284.2 eV, 286.1 eV, and 287.8 eV, which is induced by the formation of CC, CN and CN bonds, respectively [36,40]. For the N 1s spectrum, there are two peaks centered at 400.0 and 402.7 eV corresponding to the NB and CN bonds, respectively. In the O 1s spectrum, it is fitted well with only one peak centered at 530.0 eV, which is corresponded to OB bonds. The XPS results are consistent with the FTIR spectra of BCNO QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and Emission Spectra of BCNO QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of BCNO QDs under different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 3(a), BCNO QDs have two obvious absorption peaks, one peak is located at 280 nm which is originated from the intrinsic defects of BCNO phosphors, and the other one is located at 360 nm that is resulted from the carbon– and oxygen–related defects in BCNO phosphors [46]. For the photoluminescence spectra, the excitation spectra have two typical peaks which are consistent with the absorption spectra, and the emission spectra have a strong peak centered at 450 nm under excitation wavelength of 365 nm [16,36,40]. The blue emission of BCNO QDs is probably originated from two luminescence centers, which can be reflected from decay curves, as shown in Fig. S4. The double exponential fitting results demonstrate the contributions of two decay processes with the characteristic lifetime τ1 and τ2, which is related to carbon impurity and other relative defects in BCNO [55]. The BCNO QDs have very good dispersion in water as seen from the inset of Fig. 3(a). In addition, the aqueous solution of BCNO QDs displays a bright blue light under ultraviolet excitation due to high quantum yield of BCNO QDs (~17.7%). Fig. 3(b) gives the emission spectra of BCNO QDs under different excitation wavelengths, and the excitation–dependent emission can be clearly observed. The emission peak position of BCNO QDs is shifted from 440 nm to 520 nm when the excitation wavelength is increased gradually from 300 to 480 nm, which can be seen clearly from inset of Fig. 3(b). The tunable emission of BCNO QDs may be a result of quantum size effect or surface emissive sites of BCNO QDs [38,56–58]. In addition, the broad and excitation-dependent emission of BCNO QDs is similar to that of most carbon based luminescence materials, which reflects effects from both sp2 of different size and different emission sites of each sp2 clusters [59].

The effects of pH values on luminescence properties for BCNO QDs are also investigated, as shown in Fig. 4(a). The emission peak intensity of BCNO QDs is not sensitive to pH value in the range of 5–11. However, the emission intensity of BCNO QDs is decreased as the pH value is less than 5 or more than 11. The decrease of emission intensity may be induced by the influence of surface states for BCNO QDs in strong acid or base. In addition, the deprotonation of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups also can lead to the decrease of luminescence intensity [60,61]. Therefore, the pH values are maintained at 7–8 in the following experiments. Fig. 4(b) shows the elective detection of metal ions for BCNO QDs, and the effects of 16 kinds of cations, including Cu2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, K+, Cr3+, Ni2+, NH4 +, Ba2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Co2+ and Hg2+ ions were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4(b), most cations have no effect on the emission intensity of BCNO QDs except for Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions. The fluorescent intensity of BCNO QDs can be quenched by Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions. At present, the quenching effects of Cu2+ ions on luminescence are mainly attributed to the formation of a chelation between Cu2+ with N and O elements in luminescence materials [62–64]. However, there is no report about electron energy structure for BCNO with Cu2+ and detail electron transfer process to clarify the quenching effects. Fig. 4(d) shows the partial density of states for B, N, C, O, Cu2+ and configuration of BCNO with Cu2+ ion after energy optimization. According to our previous calculation of BN and BCNO [65], the B and N elements are mainly contributed to conduction and valence bands, respectively. For BCNO, impurity levels are produced between valence and conduction bands which are mainly from the contribution of C and O elements. For BCNO with Cu2+ ion, the Cu2+ ion is stable directly above the C atom with a distance of 1.929 Å (Fig. S5(a)) after energy optimization. In addition, new energy levels from Cu2+ ion appear with high density of states. One of Cu2+ relative levels is located at −1.8 eV below Fermi level, which is overlapped with C related levels and possess higher density of states, which can be served as donor energy levels. Another energy level of Cu2+ ions passes through the Fermi level, which is very important to the electron transport process. As the electrons are excited by incident photon, the electrons will be transferred from valence band or carbon related levels to conduction band of BCNO. For BCNO with Cu2+ ion, the relative energy levels of Cu2+ ions which are above Fermi level can be served as acceptor impurity levels to accept some excited electrons. However, the accepted electrons by Cu2+ ions probably transfer to other copper ions instead of jumping back to valence band or carbon related levels, which leads to the decrease of emission intensity for BCNO with increasing concentration of Cu2+ ions. Moreover, the emission peak of BCNO QDs have a red shift from 420 nm to 440 nm with increasing Cu2+ ion concentration, which also reflects the appearance of new levels from Cu2+ ions. As a result, the luminescence quenching of BCNO QDs takes place as the concentration of Cu2+ ions reaches a critical threshold. In addition, we have done the time resolved measurement of BCNO QDs and BCNO QDs with Cu2+ ions (100 μM), as shown in Fig. S4. From the fitting results of decay curves, it can be seen that the lifetime of BCNO QDs is decreased with addition of Cu2+ ions, which is induced by the electrons transfer from BCNO to Cu2+ ions and result in the decrease of luminescence intensity for BCNO QDs.

BCNO QDs can also be used to detect the concentration of Hg2+ ions in aqueous solution according to the quenching effects of Hg2+ ions on luminescence of BCNO QDs. To determine the limit concentration of Hg2+ ion, different concentrations of Hg2+ ions were added into the aqueous solution of BCNO QDs, and the effects of Hg2+ ions on emission spectra are shown in Fig. 4(e). The Hg2+ ions have a strong luminescence quenching on BCNO QDs, and the emission intensity of BCNO QDs is decreased with increasing concentration of Hg2+ ions. The detection limit of BCNO QDs for Hg2+ ion can be as less as 10 nM (nmol/L), which is at the high level compared with other quantum dots [66–68]. The quenching effect of Hg2+ ion on BCNO QDs is also induced by the electrons transfer from the BCNO QDs to Hg2+ ions [69,70]. It can be also reflected by the decrease of lifetime for BCNO QDs with Hg2+ ions (Fig. S4). Fig. 4(f) gives the partial density of states and configuration of BCNO with Hg2+ ion. The Hg2+ ion is also stable directly above the C atom with a distance of 2.398 Å after energy optimization (Fig. S5(b)). From the PDOS of BCNO with Hg2+ ion, it can be seen that small energy levels of Hg2+ ion appear below Fermi level, and more impurity levels are observed above Fermi level 0.5 eV and 4.0 eV which can be served as acceptor energy levels. After photon excitation, some excited electrons from carbon or other impurity levels will jump back to relative energy levels of Hg2+ ion and the electrons will be transmitted to other mercury ions, which lead to the decrease of emission intensity for BCNO QDs. The luminescence quenching of BCNO QDs is occurred with increasing concentration of Hg2+ ions. From Fig. 4(c) and (e), it can be found that BCNO QDs are more sensitive to Cu2+ ion since less Cu2+ can result in the same decrease of emission intensity, which is probably originated from less distance (1.929 Å) and different density of states for BCNO with Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions, particularly with respect to the high density of state for Cu2+ ions crossing the Fermi level. As a result, the specific selectivity of BCNO QDs for Cu2+and Hg2+ ions may be originated from their special electronic structures and the luminescence quenching effects are mainly induced by the electrons transition process between BCNO QDs and Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions [71].

We also calculated the effects of Mg2+ and K+ ions as typical ions which have little influence on emission spectra of BCNO QDs, as shown in Figs. S6 and S7, respectively. From the configuration of Mg2+ ion with BCNO after energy optimization, it can be seen that the Mg2+ ion is not located on the above of C atom and far away from the BCNO, which leads to the very weak interaction and no obvious influence on emission spectra of BCNO. In addition, there are no impurity levels across the Fermi level, and the overlapping of partial density of states between Mg2+ ion and BCNO is low, resulting in weak influence on emission spectra of BCNO. For K+ ion, it can not be stably located above the B, C, N or O atom after energy optimization, which results in little interaction between K+ ion and BCNO and no influence on luminescence property of BCNO.

Ascorbic acid (AA) is an important part of human body, accurate determination of ascorbic acid is essential to food quality and biological fields. Unfortunately, AA has no fluorescence response on BCNO QDs (shown in Fig. 5(a)). Fortunately, the K2Cr2O7 has a fluorescence quenching effect on BCNO QDs, and the emission intensity of BCNO QDs as a function of the concentrations of K2Cr2O7 is shown in Fig. S8. The emission intensity of BCNO QDs is decreased with increasing concentration of K2Cr2O7. The quenching mechanism is caused by the inner filter effect [58] since K2Cr2O7 has three broad absorption peaks centered at 260, 360 and 440 nm, which has a large spectra overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of BCNO QDs (as shown in Fig. 5(b)). K2Cr2O7 not only absorbs the excitation light of BCNO QDs, but also absorbs the emission of BCNO QDs. The absorption of K2Cr2O7 becomes weak with decreased concentration of Cr6+ ions, which weakens the inner filter effect, as shown in Fig. S9 [72,73]. The Cr6+ ions in K2Cr2O7 have an intense oxidizability which can be used to detect the AA, because Cr6+ ion can be reduced to Cr3+ by AA while Cr3+ ion has no response to the photoluminescence of BCNO QDs. Therefore, we used the BCNO–Cr6+ mixture to detect the AA since Cr6+ ions can be reduced by AA [72,74,75]. Fig. 5(c) shows the emission spectra of BCNO–Cr6+ mixture (fixed concentration of K2Cr2O7 (50 μM)) with adding different concentrations of AA (0, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200 μM). The relationship between emission peak intensity and AA concentration is shown in Fig. 5(d), and the linear curve with the fitting equation is as follows:
(1)
F = 1809 ∗
AA
+ 6.56 6E5
R 2
= 0.978
The added AA recovers the fluorescence intensity of BCNO QDs and the BCNO–Cr6+–AA can be served as an on-off-on chemical sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fingerprint Identification and Organic Luminescence Film Applications
ce:section.ce:para: The BCNO QDs also have good dispersion in organic solvents such as PVA or PMMA (polymethyl methacrylate), which can be applied to fingerprint identification, as shown in Fig. 6(a)–(c). The fingerprint has made full use of BCNO QDs–PVA solution, and the mixture was nearly colorless in daylight after drying in ambient condition for about one minute (Fig. 6(a)). However, bright blue fingerprint features can be clearly observed upon irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 6(c)). Therefore, the BCNO–PVA solution can provide a simple and fast method for fingerprint identification, which has great potential applications in criminal investigation, encryption and security design. In addition, organic luminescence films can be fabricated by drying the BCNO QDs–PVA solution, and they have a bright blue emission under UV lamp, which can be used for light–emitting polymer materials and organic luminescence devices.

In this paper, we successfully synthesized blue emitting BCNO QDs with a high QY of 17.7% via the template of SBA–15. A new modulated chemical sensor based on BCNO QDs is developed in detecting the Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions as they have a fluorescence quenching on BCNO QDs, and the electron transition processes between BCNO and Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions are provided according to the theoretical calculation results. We also demonstrated the on–off–on fluorescent method for detecting the Cr6+ (off) and AA (on) based on the inner filter effect of fluorescence. The detection of AA by BCNO QDs can provide a promising method in biological fields. Furthermore, the BCNO QDs can also play an important role in fingerprint identification and organic luminescence films which have great potential applications in information areas and organic luminescence devices, respectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51771068, 21603052, 51671079, 51771067, 51571123, 51671108), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. E2018202082), Financial Support for Scientific and Technological Activities of Returnees from Abroad (CL201606), Excellent Youth Foundation for Institution of Higher Learning of Hebei Province (No. YQ2014017), and Innovation Fund for Excellent Youth of Hebei University of Technology (No. 2015005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.05.099.
